sentences.text: Weber.
Regina, we were now in America.
Yes.
And you're living with your father, the three girls.
It's not good.
Not the three girls, because my sister [NON-ENGLISH] got married in two weeks.
[LAUGHS]
Two weeks, and now it's two girls.
Two girls, yes.
[NON-ENGLISH] is married, Berta-- that's Berta--
Berta is married.
Well, we-- Izabel certainly tried.
I didn't try as much as Izabel.
To get along my father.
I was very angry because he kept on telling me, you killed your mother.
And I said, if anybody, you killed our mother.
And I don't feel that way anymore.
[LAUGHS] The Germans-- nothing to do really with my father.
He didn't want this to happen.
No human being wanted this to happen.
And I'm sorry it happened to him.
But I guess we-- the girls and my brother-- had to save our lives.
And he threw us out.
He couldn't take us.
Well, I was 17 and 1/2.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they--
--always said-- we had-- my brother was here already.
And he took one suitcase for the three of us.
And we never knew where are we going to sleep at night because the US Army was coming home.
And we couldn't even get a hotel room.
But then it-- I went to work.
And I didn't know where I'm going to go for dinner, or where-- we ate in the hotel room some sandwiches, you know.
Yes.
No, my sister wasn't, just-- my brother was working, I think.
I was on 57th Street in a very fancy couturier house.
And I was in the sewing department.
I did some interesting things.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, with Phillip's-- what Phillip made and I made together, we were just about able to very mildly support ourselves and feel guilty about our father--
--feel that we-- that we left him.
Yes, felt for years.
[SIGHS] Yes, there was no apartment built or available in 1945.
[SIGHS] You won't believe it, because now it's really just-- if it's there, it's a whorehouse, the Hotel Dixie on, on 42nd Street.
But we managed.
We only stayed-- they-- you could only stay in a hotel for four days because they had to change the people.
Anyway, eventually, Izabel met a Hungarian psychologist, a male, who wanted our story in But we decided against it because we said, that's going to just end up with smelly fish the next today.
We don't want anything like that.
And he found us a hotel-- Chelsea.
It's a very famous hotel.
But the owner was Hungarian.
And he was Hungarian.
And we lived there for about a year or two.
And then we started to meet people, very interesting Americans.
And we started fluently to speak English in six months.
Because that's what we wanted to be, Americans, you know?
We loved America.
It was just to made to order for us.
We were worldly--
Yes, and we knew them.
And then we ended up in Greenwich Village on the best street, 10th Street--
--between Fifth and Sixth Avenue.
Well, Lenny Bernstein lived across the hall-- Evans, the actor.
And we became friendly with musicians and artists.
And I guess we were born bohemians.
And that was due to my mother, you know.
We were and are slightly bohemian, you know. but in our brains to live good lives, you know.
[LAUGHS] Didn't marry an artist or-- I knew that I don't want to, you know?
And [NON-ENGLISH] was married to a rich man.
And she helped us tremendously.
We still adore my brother-in-law.
And [NON-ENGLISH] is like my mother, a goody-- good person.
And she's always helped us with my child, with--
Well--
I ended up-- the business went out of business on 57th Street.
So I ended up on Seventh Avenue, which I didn't want to, because my brother said, you're not going to go out with anybody from Seventh Avenue.
That's, you know, lower people than [LAUGHS] us, you know.
Yeah.
And I got this job with Anne Klein.
And Bob's brother was a salesman.
His whole family, for three generations--
Anne Klein was a designer.
I knew who she was.
And my husband's family are in the Garment Center for three-- for three generations.
So his brother came and met me.
And he says, boy, it would be nice to introduce you to my brother Bob.
And he says, here is a telephone number.
Call up.
I said, Harold, I never call a man.
They call me.
[LAUGHS] So he called up on the telephone.
And I spoke to Bob.
And I felt something.
I was engaged to Nathan's Hot Dogs son, but I didn't love him.
And Bob's voice-- Nathan's [LAUGHS] Hot Dogs son-- [LAUGHS]
Mm-hmm, and I didn't have one single penny to my name.
Anyway, I met Bob on a blind date.
And that was it.
We fell in love.
Something-- Bob-- we were drawn to each other because he's a very unusual man.
He became my father and my mother.
And he has had a very difficult life with me.
But obviously, good enough if he-- if he stayed with me all these years.
It's going to be 45 years next week--
--that we've been married.
Thank you.
But it's been difficult.
I've been very sick.
Especially after I became pregnant and I had my child, I had a total breakdown.
Yolanda.
Oh, well, she is-- also, I feel that my mother is alive, you know?
Because she, too, is a humanitarian and works with children who need her help, you know.
She is a PhD, clinical psychologist.
And she is a therapist.
And she just went into group therapy.
And the hospital is very happy with her that she started this group therapy.
And she didn't get married young.
She was an artist first.
And, you know, we let her do whatever she wanted to.
And then one day she says, Mommy, I'm never going to make a living as an artist.
So she went up to Columbia University.
Needless to say, she was an A student.
And they took her in this small program of 14 people.
And she became a clinical psychologist.
And she only married after that, which hurt me terribly.
I felt maybe she'll never get married.
Oh.
[SIGHS] And she always had problems, and she still has problems.
Yes.
I became terribly depressed.
I couldn't take care of my child.
I was hospitalized for a breakdown.
Or you could call it--
Nervous breakdown.
Or you could call it--
Postpartum depression.
And I was hospitalized for 10 months.
And then I remained with that same psychiatrist that I met there.
And there were no pills.
There was nothing.
But he saw me for another 10 years.
And he saw me and my child.
He made me just that strong.
I always say, I brought up my child with the skin of my teeth because I was always sick.
And my husband, thank God, is a healthy fellow.
And he tried to be mother and father to my child.
But when she saw me sitting depressed for months, she couldn't cope with it.
We sent her to camp.
And we did everything to try to make her life normal.
But it couldn't be normal with a mother who wasn't.
I was depressed a lot, an awful lot, and in treatment all the time, and spending a lot of money to keep me going.
And we still do. 50 years later, and a lot of our money goes into medication and treatment.
But--
My age.
I think that I was, you know, youngest.
And I, I definitely believe in that, that--
--the trauma was much more distinct in me, even though I've been trying to deny it all these years, you know?
It's inside of me.
And the best psychiatrist, the most expensive psychiatrist, couldn't take my-- what I feel in my heart and make me well.
But I have to be grateful that I am as well as I am.
And my daughter, finally-- she didn't want to get married until she got her PhD.
So she was engaged for a year, which upset me terribly.
But I never said anything to her.
David.
And he is a, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a pediatric endocrinologist.
And he works in the same hospital in Cincinnati, the Children's Hospital, which is a very famous hospital.
So something must have been going right.
That's what everybody says.
But--
A little credit, yes, occasionally.
But yes, she's doing-- she's helping people.
She is the most wonderful mother.
And so is David-- a wonderful father.
They have two.
They have Hannah, who is five years old, brilliant and gorgeous.
And there's Benjamin, who is out of this world.
I never heard anybody so bright in my life-- and gorgeous.
But I have trouble.
I have trouble with Yolanda because I am still so involved in my hurt.
It doesn't want to leave me.
No matter how much therapy I had, for 45 years, it's there.
And it's Auschwitz.
And I'd love to get rid of it.
But, I mean, I'm too old already.
I have to learn and live with my, my-- I'm a crippled person, I feel of myself as such.
Even though in-between, you know, I, I designed clothes and I sold it on Madison Avenue, and I, I think I'm somewhat of a decorator, you know?
I did beautiful things, and we traveled.
And I must have given a lot of things to my husband because he wouldn't have stayed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I still feel like a victim, even though-- I mean, I have-- I always say this to people because they look at me.
And I say, yes, I have everything in life-- a loving husband, children, grandchildren, my son-in-law.
But I don't have [SOBBING] myself.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] I don't.
I'm not trying to impress what-- I've been left, I think, a little more crippled than some other people.
This is my feeling about myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's a difficult life, even though I have everything.
And now I'm getting older.
You know what happens in life.
Things become more exaggerated as you get older.
And I'm-- I'd like to come to some terms, definitely, with my daughter, you know?
That I-- I just couldn't tell her anything about Auschwitz when she was growing up.
You never talked about it?
Never.
[SOBBING] Never a word.
And Bob didn't want to either.
And then when she became a teenager, she says, Mommy.
But there are ways [SOBBING] to tell a child in her own way.
I said, but Yolanda, [SOBBING] I didn't have the capacity to do that.
[SOBBING] And I've been feeling guilty about her.
And I tried my best.
And I think she's a wonderful, fabulous girl, and she's living up to my family.
And that's very important to me, to-- especially to live up to my mother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] humanity.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
She-- but she has more selfishness, thank God, more normal selfishness.
She cares about her children.
And she does things--
Yes, but she also cared about a lot of other people, which I didn't believe up until I was showed by this therapist that maybe your mother did also a disservice by being so good to everybody.
It's hard for me to accept, you know.
Right.
Because human beings are not saints.
But we were brought up with the idea.
And I think I still feel that she was-- there's nobody in the world like my mother.
There is just nobody.
I'm very grateful that I had a mother [SOBBING] who taught me to be good to human beings.
And I chose a husband who is-- feels the same way I do.
And I brought up a child after all that suffering from the Germans.
We remained totally human.
And they're going back to killing people again.
The Germans.
There is a lot of anti-Semitism there in Germany.
And I don't trust them, you know, the Germans.
But--
And-- I went back once.
And I wouldn't go back again.
I had to go back.
Something drew me there.
And it was very difficult.
And I will never go back there again, never.
Well, just the idea of the catharsis, you know, of seeing not many Jews in Budapest.
Only my aunt was alive from a family of 500 people.
And they will kill us just like the Germans.
Not all of them, I suppose.
But my experience was very bad with the Hungarians.
And then worst is Auschwitz and the Germans.
No, I couldn't do that.
Because I always felt-- [SOBBING] my mother loved yellow roses.
She was what they call a Feinschmecker, you know?
She loved beautiful--
--things.
Refined.
And I always have this dream [SOBBING] that I go and she has-- she's buried, but she isn't.
And I'm going to her grave.
And I put a yellow rose on there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this is not to happen.
But my daughter and I, we send roses to each other, 12 beautiful roses on every birthday.
And my husband brings me roses on all holidays and everything.
[SOBBING] But I'm still a cripple.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You can't take Auschwitz out of a person, out of me.
Maybe I was just born too sensitive.
But I, I can't help that.
I mean, I'm getting all the help, America's foremost psychiatrists and everything.
And there is just a limit of how much they can help me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should become stronger because my life is coming to a fruition, you know?
And I try very hard.
I really work with the therapist, with myself, with my husband.
And now I will try to work more with Yolanda.
But I think with all that, I have to accept the fact that I will suffer all my life.
Because if it didn't help for 50 years, it would be childish to think that I'll just get out of it.
My depressions, yes, yes, yes.
I'm doing better now.
But I'm a very talented person.
I was never--
No, because-- not much, very little.
Because I think that the, the medication kind of takes away dreaming, you know?
Yes, yes, yes.
Oh, I hate them.
I run away.
And I call them Hund like they call their dog, you know.
That's a Hund-- a German word, 'Hund,' a dog.
I hate all dogs.
And it seems that I am so frightened that when the telephone rings, I go-- shake.
When a siren or anything, or a car stops rapidly, I, I get all upset.
Yes, oh I--
[SIGHS] Yeah, it will never leave me, never.
I have no control over it, absolutely none.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would like to feel better.
And I'm going to work at it.
But I don't know, because I have suffered so much.
Yes, about every three months we go over there.
And Bob and I are very good with them.
Although I don't do physically so much, but I am-- they love us--
--because we are very good grandparents, you know?
Yolanda will-- you know, and creative grandparents, you know.
Oh, no, not at all.
We take Hannah out for a special luncheon when we're there.
She's a little young lady.
And Benjamin, I mean, he gets on the telephone.
He's-- he was two years old.
And he says-- he calls me Coco.
And he says, Coco, you're pretty.
Or I told him the other day-- he answers the telephone-- and always his father answers.
So I said, hi, David.
He says, I'm not David.
I am Benjamin.
And it's just amazing, but--
Yes, because I don't have all that responsibility.
But it's not enough for me.
I want to love unconditionally anything possible with my depressions, you know?
I am a loving, giving human being.
But these damn depressions just hold me back.
And I don't want it anymore.
So what choice do I have?
To try to live as healthy as I can.
But I'm not satisfied.
I'm happy-- Your daughter, does she live in New York?
No, they lived in Cincinnati.
And so we fly up there.
And, you know, she has a big household.
And, you know, we just are the nice grandparents.
And they would like to see us more.
But it's very difficult for me to get myself together and go more often because of the problems I have.
Well, I am getting much older.
And I still haven't made any peace with God.
After Auschwitz, yes.
There is no God for me.
It's not a happy thought, you know?
It would be nice to believe in something.
But I believe still with all my heart and strength in humanity because I've met some wonderful people in this country who I only met because I came from a family where my mother was so special.
And I guess I believe in humanity.
No.
I don't--
I can't do anything, no.
I don't do anything.
And that's what you say is the anger in me.
But--
No.
Not for my mother, no.
I don't light candles.
It's too painful.
I couldn't look at a flickering life-- light because that's like life going out when you see a flickering light.
I just-- I can't do it.
I have a sense of beauty and something about life that I, [SIGHS] I don't want to face the ugliness.
And--
--life--
His family wasn't religious.
He wasn't bar mitzvahed.
He was-- came from a very good family.
They're wonderful people.
And they help me a lot.
Maybe more.
But they're not all religious, you know.
Oh--
--I couldn't be more Jewish-- or Bob.
Me and my daughter, very interesting-- my daughter and her husband, who liked a little hippiness in the '60s, they're conservative.
They belong to a Reformed synagogue.
She lights candles every Friday.
And she goes with her children and her husband to the shul.
And I am very happy about that.
In spite [LAUGHS] of us--
--you know?
They're searching for something.
And it's good.
I think it's healthy, and it's good.
That I can't do it, I'm still denying something.
That's all possible.
It's all possible.
Oh, yes.
We've been-- South America.
I became a Mayan student when we used to go 10 times in Mexico and take Yolanda with us everywhere.
She's traveled around the world, you know.
We've been in Egypt.
We've been in China.
We've been--
Well, my brother was living there.
And I was so proud of him, you know, doing his work for--
At that time, he was head of PR at Ben-Gurion University.
But he traveled all over the world to collect money for it-- not just the university, for all Israel causes, you know, and Jewish causes.
And he has devoted his life.
He's retired now.
But his whole life-- he never was a big moneymaker.
He made a living.
But we're very proud of him.
Jewish people, yes.
And he's a beautiful human being.
Well, it's--
--it's, it's because I am really so Jewish.
Even-- I'm trying to deny it, you know?
I mean, I-- my heritage was beautiful.
I can't live like that anymore.
But my heart and soul is totally Jewish.
And on--
Yes, but I didn't go in.
I waited outside.
I never saw a movie.
I-- if there is a Nazi appearing on television, Bob cuts it off because I can't take it.
I can't take anything that has to do with the past.
I am totally locked in.
It's very difficult.
But I can't come out of myself.
No, no.
He's a friend of my brother's and my sister's.
And I-- no, I never would read anything about the Holocaust.
Izabel said, well, why don't you come with me?
Yesterday they had a meeting for Anne Frank.
I said, I don't go anywhere.
But I am going to appear today.
It was so difficult.
Thank you.
Thank you.
